agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Critical SQL injection vulnerability — unsanitized user input concatenated directly into query string from a public-facing API."
  },
  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "SQL Injection via raw string interpolation of HTTP request parameters",
      "risk": "Full database compromise: an attacker can read, modify, or delete any data, bypass authentication, or execute administrative operations depending on DB permissions.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — query construction via f-string",
      "reproduction": "Send request with status_filter = `' OR 1=1; DROP TABLE deliveries; --`. The resulting query becomes: SELECT * FROM deliveries WHERE customer_id = '...' AND status = '' OR 1=1; DROP TABLE deliveries; --'. This executes arbitrary SQL. Even simpler: customer_id = `' OR '1'='1` returns all customers' deliveries, leaking data across tenant boundaries.",
      "fix": "Use parameterized queries: `query = \"SELECT * FROM deliveries WHERE customer_id = ? AND status = ?\"; result = db_conn.execute(query, (customer_id, status_filter))`",
      "time_to_fix": "5 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Security, data isolation, regulatory compliance (PCI/GDPR if delivery data contains PII)"
    },
    {
      "title": "SELECT * returns unbounded columns and rows — data leak and performance risk",
      "risk": "Exposes internal columns (e.g. internal_notes, driver_ssn, cost) to the public API response. No LIMIT clause means a broad filter can return millions of rows, causing OOM or denial of service.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — SELECT * with no LIMIT",
      "reproduction": "Call with a customer_id that has 500k deliveries and status = 'pending'. The query returns every column of every matching row in one fetch, exhausting memory.",
      "fix": "Enumerate only the columns the API contract requires (e.g. `SELECT id, status, estimated_arrival`) and add a LIMIT/OFFSET or cursor-based pagination.",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "API performance SLA, data minimization principle"
    },
    {
      "title": "No authorization check — any caller can query any customer_id",
      "risk": "Horizontal privilege escalation: a logged-in customer can pass another customer's ID and retrieve their delivery data, violating tenant isolation.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — customer_id taken directly from request without authz validation",
      "reproduction": "Authenticate as customer A, call endpoint with customer_id=B. All of customer B's deliveries are returned.",
      "fix": "Derive customer_id from the authenticated session/token rather than accepting it as a query parameter, or validate that the authenticated user is authorized to access the requested customer_id before executing the query.",
      "time_to_fix": "20 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Authorization, multi-tenant data isolation, GDPR/compliance"
    }
  ],
  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No input validation on status_filter",
      "description": "Even after fixing SQL injection, status_filter should be validated against an allowlist of known statuses (e.g. 'pending', 'in_transit', 'delivered') to prevent nonsensical queries and improve observability.",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — status_filter parameter"
    },
    {
      "title": "No error handling around db_conn.execute",
      "description": "Database connection errors, timeouts, or invalid queries will propagate as unhandled exceptions, likely returning a 500 with a raw stack trace to the public caller — leaking implementation details.",
      "location": "search_deliveries() — db_conn.execute(query)"
    }
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    "Add a query timeout to prevent long-running queries from holding connections.",
    "Add an index on (customer_id, status) if not already present to support this access pattern.",
    "Log query execution for audit trail purposes (with parameterized form, never the interpolated SQL)."
  ],
  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "This code is exploitable the instant it is reachable from the public internet. Any deployment exposes the entire database.",
    "rollback_capability": "Rollback is safe but does not undo damage if the endpoint was exploited before rollback.",
    "backward_compatibility": "Switching to parameterized queries is backward-compatible from the caller's perspective.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No logging or metrics on this endpoint. SQL injection attempts would go undetected.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": true,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": ["auth_security: endpoint lacks authorization and is vulnerable to SQL injection"]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert the commit or disable the route entirely until the parameterized query fix is applied."
  },
  "escalations": [],
  "confidence": {
    "level": 99,
    "high_confidence_areas": ["SQL injection is unambiguous and trivially exploitable", "Missing authz is evident from the function signature and described call site"],
    "low_confidence_areas": ["Cannot verify DB driver specifics or ORM layer without seeing the full codebase"],
    "assumptions": ["db_conn.execute runs raw SQL as shown (no hidden ORM sanitization)", "customer_id and status_filter are passed through from HTTP params without middleware sanitization"]
  }
}

content.thinking: SQL injection vulnerability, clear as day.
